title: A Look at Rogue Dungeon
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BfLT1TlZInE

Daniel (@dungeondive) reviews Albert Danish and Chad Minnichia's *Rogue Dungeon* — a Game Crafter card-based roguelike that he openly says he'd pay to help develop if the channel had the money. The praise is unguarded: this is, for him, one of the best small-box card-based crawls available.

Five classes (Crusader, Rogue, Dwarf Miner, Sorcerer, Elf Ranger), each with primary-stat-driven combat and a tiny pool of class skills. Each run is a five-floor descent against one of four final bosses (red dragon, balrog, lich king, storm giant) — Daniel has yet to reach the boss after three attempts. The standout mechanism is the yellow-room system: yellow encounter cards become *persistent* rooms on that floor, meaning backtracking is genuinely risky and route planning matters. "It doesn't feel like flipping through a deck — it feels like exploring a dungeon."

Two complaints. First, the art direction is incoherent — pixel-art-adjacent CGI, hand-drawn fantasy, and stone-textured cards all share table space without a unifying style. Second, with only five cards per coloured-room deck and one floor needing exactly five, every full exploration sees every card; a small expansion of swap-in cards would fix it.

----

Daniel argues a more focused art director could push this from cult favourite to stone-cold classic. How much does visual cohesion really matter to a card-based dungeon crawl's reputation?
